A group of angry investors is suing Fox News, claiming that its pandering t=
o right-wing conspiracies saddled the company with legal costs they should =
have avoided. Murdoch is claiming free speech outweighs fiduciary responsib=
ility, and many in the media are hoping he wins.

Last Friday, I dropped by the Leonard L. Williams Justice Center=E2=80=94th=
e Wilmington courthouse where Fox News chose to settle its defamation case =
with Dominion Voting Systems for $787 million in April 2023=E2=80=94to obse=
rve a new Fox legal battle attracting far less fanfare: a lawsuit accusing =
Rupert Murdoch and his lieutenants of breaching fiduciary duties by panderi=
ng to Donald Trump and nurturing a right-wing audience with a steady diet o=
f misinformation.

Fox shareholders claim the board ignored clear defamation risks, saddling t=
he company with astronomical legal costs =C3=A1 la the Dominion settlement.=
 A hearing to decide whether the case proceeds allowed Joel Friedlander, th=
e shareholders=E2=80=99 attorney, to spotlight internal communications at F=
ox, including a post-2020 election email from Murdoch to Fox News C.E.O.
Suzanne Scott acknowledging many of the batshit comments on the network bef=
ore noting, as if with a shrug, =E2=80=9CLots of sane Fox viewers still bel=
ieve in Trump.=E2=80=9D

With Trump poised to return to 1600 Pennsylvania Ave. and pledging retribut=
ion against foes real and imagined, the air in Wilmington felt particularly=
 charged. But William Savitt, Fox=E2=80=99s attorney at Wachtell, cautioned=
 the court against viewing defamation threats as anything out of the ordina=
ry in the media sphere. He argued for a careful understanding of what actua=
lly constitutes a red flag and necessitates boardroom intervention, lest th=
e industry be besieged by copycat litigation. =E2=80=9CThe premise that Fox=
 is doing things radically different,=E2=80=9D Savitt claimed, =E2=80=9Cis =
simply not true.=E2=80=9D

What if Savitt has a point? Ever since Fox News found itself mired in contr=
oversy over election fraud claims, it=E2=80=99s consistently waved the bann=
er of free speech. And yet, for the most part,
other media entities have shrugged off Fox=E2=80=99s predicaments as unique=
, with outlets like The New York Times even suggesting ( https://www.nytime=
s.com/2022/03/13/business/media/fox-news-first-amendment-sullivan.html?utm_=

ail_action&utm_source=3Dcustomer.io&utm_term=3Df6c60617da42abd12a ), back i=
n 2022, that the survival of First Amendment protections for media companie=
s might hinge on Fox=E2=80=99s defeat in court. Supposedly, such protection=
s would only continue to get the public=E2=80=99s buy-in if the worst of th=

Now, however, the entertainment and media industries face their own looming=

 9495, a bill just passed by the House that would allow the treasury secret=
ary to strip

ences if, say, a famous actor speaks critically during an Oscars speech abo=
ut the president=E2=80=99s approach to the Israel-Hamas war?

arr, may start scrutinizing the character of licensed television stations m=

tly underestimate his charm and sense of humor, and may have a hard time re=
sisting the weird undertow that still accompanies any member of America=E2=
=80=99s most mythic political dynasty. If he
does secure Senate approval, Kennedy=E2=80=99s tenure and all-hours media a=
ppetite will ignite unpredictable firestorms=E2=80=94even for a Trump admin=
istration.

For starters, Kennedy has set his sights on the pharmaceutical industry, a =
juggernaut of lobbying and advertising with annual expenditures topping $20=
 billion=E2=80=94more than two-thirds of which floods television networks, =
the province of elderly eyeballs. Alongside Elon Musk, Kennedy has floated =
the notion of banning Big Pharma=E2=80=99s TV ads=E2=80=94a move that could=
 potentially cripple linear television, as Mark Cuban noted ( https://x.com=
/mcuban/status/1859653929447109084?utm_campaign=3DWhat+I%27m+Hearing%2B+-+S=
UBSCRIBERS+%2811%2F26%2F24%29&utm_content=3DWhat+I%27m+Hearing%2B+-+SUBSCRI=
BERS+%2810%2F15%2F24%29&utm_medium=3Demail_action&utm_source=3Dcustomer.io&=
utm_term=3Df6c60617da42abd12a ) the other day. While a total ban might buck=
le under First Amendment scrutiny, Kennedy could nevertheless aim to curb t=
he sector=E2=80=99s marketing clout. Notably, the
Biden administration just concluded its own initiatives for prescription dr=
ug ads, setting new disclosure requirements ( https://www.reginfo.gov/publi=
c/do/eAgendaViewRule?pubId=3D202304&RIN=3D0910-AG27&utm_campaign=3DWhat+I%2=
7m+Hearing%2B+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%2811%2F26%2F24%29&utm_content=3DWhat+I%27m+Hea=
ring%2B+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%2810%2F15%2F24%29&utm_medium=3Demail_action&utm_sour=
ce=3Dcustomer.io&utm_term=3Df6c60617da42abd12a ), but Kennedy could push th=
ose much further and undercut the appeal and viability of such advertising.

$(ad3_title) ( https://ad.doubleclick.net/ddm/trackclk/N1956727.4235865PUCK=
NEWS/B32797622.409127550;dc_trk_aid=3D600983887;dc_trk_cid=3D226429236;dc_l=
at=3D;dc_rdid=3D;tag_for_child_directed_treatment=3D;tfua=3D;ltd=3D;dc_tdv=
=3D1?utm_campaign=3DWhat+I%27m+Hearing%2B+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%2811%2F26%2F24%29&=
utm_content=3DWhat+I%27m+Hearing%2B+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%2810%2F15%2F24%29&utm_me=
dium=3Demail_action&utm_source=3Dcustomer.io&utm_term=3Df6c60617da42abd12a =
)

Kennedy could be disruptive in other ways, too. He=E2=80=99s preternaturall=
y at home in a courtroom: R.F.K. Jr. led his Children=E2=80=99s Health Defe=
nse in suits against top Biden health officials, like Anthony Fauci, over a=
ttempts to suppress Covid vaccine skepticism on social media. Then there wa=
s his antitrust case challenging major news organizations, like the BBC and=
 TheWashington Post, for their roles in a health-focused, fact-checking con=
sortium. Some of these suits failed after courts ruled his group lacked sta=
nding to sue, but that would be less of a problem if government agencies un=
der his purview decided to try their own cases.

It=E2=80=99s even possible that HHS could stand up to proposed mergers in t=
he media industry, similar to the way the Departments of Transportation and=
 Defense began scrutinizing M&A under Biden. (The
agencies took lead roles in blocking the Jet Blue-Spirit merger and Lockhee=
d Martin=E2=80=99s purchase of Aerojet Rocketdyne.) If Kennedy can position=
 himself as a champion of America=E2=80=99s health=E2=80=94still a big if=
=E2=80=94his new perch could allow him greater leverage to intercede in a m=
ultitude of ways.

Finally, HHS is a cornerstone in federal scientific research, a foundation =
often utilized by journalists covering major institutions and corporate beh=
aviors. If Kennedy were to tamper with this research=E2=80=94by curtailing =
it, limiting access, or skewing it to fit particular ideological leanings=
=E2=80=94it could erode the factual bedrock upon which journalists build th=
eir stories. That, in turn, could expose media entities to libel risks, as =
the defensibility of their coverage could be compromised.

Not Dystopian, But Murdochian

Of course, no one knows what Trump 2.0 will bring. Some fear we=E2=80=99re =
headed into a new, autocratic era, but I think we=E2=80=99re bound for some=
thing less dystopian but potentially more fraught: an atmosphere in which m=
edia entities retain independence and perhaps even flourish financially, an=
d yet proceed cautiously while prioritizing self-preservation, becoming les=
s daring and effective in the process. There=E2=80=99s some basis for this =
outcome in the media landscape of the U.K., a region of fascinating contrad=
ictions.

Britain is renowned for =E2=80=9Clibel tourism,=E2=80=9D thanks to defamati=
on laws heavily skewed in favor of the plaintiff. Journalists often find th=
emselves in the precarious position of having to substantiate truth in cour=
t, as opposed to their subjects having to demonstrate falsity in the U.S. M=
oreover, the U.K. pioneered the use of
super-injunctions, a legal maneuver that sometimes prevents the press from =
even detailing allegations against powerful figures. In other words, it=E2=
=80=99s a dangerous country to publish anything sensitive that might tarnis=
h someone=E2=80=99s reputation.

Ironically, alongside this restrictive legal framework thrives a vibrant ta=
bloid culture. British publications famously pursue celebrities and sensati=
onalize gossip, prioritizing scandal over substance and inflaming class and=
 racial distinctions, while relying on the relatively modest damages awarde=
d in successful libel suits in the U.K., and potential landmines lurking in=
 discovery. This discourages all but the most well-funded targets. Despite =
the tabloids=E2=80=99 commercial success, this is not the kind of journalis=
m that cements a reputation as a formidable fourth estate.

Maybe it=E2=80=99s a window into Trump 2.0: fewer investigative outlets to =
hold power to account, but more entertainment. You might even call it
Murdochian.

That=E2=80=99s it for today. Happy Thanksgiving, I=E2=80=99ll see you on Mo=
nday.

Matt

         <tr class=3D"replica" name=3D"s16">
          <td style=3D"padding-bottom: 36px; color: #000000; font-size: 16p=
x; line-height: 30px; margin-top: 0; margin-bottom: 0;">Kennedy could be di=
sruptive in other ways, too. He=E2=80=99s preternaturally at home in a cour=
troom: R.F.K. Jr. led his Children=E2=80=99s Health Defense in suits agains=
t top Biden health officials, like <b>Anthony Fauci</b>, over attempts to s=
uppress Covid vaccine skepticism on social media. Then there was his antitr=
ust case challenging major news organizations, like the BBC and <i>TheWashi=
ngton Post</i>, for their roles in a health-focused, fact-checking consorti=
um. Some of these suits failed after courts ruled his group lacked standing=
 to sue, but that would be less of a problem if government agencies under h=
is purview decided to try their own cases. <br/><br/>It=E2=80=99s even poss=
ible that HHS could stand up to proposed mergers in the <i>media industry</=
i>, similar to the way the Departments of Transportation and Defense began =
scrutinizing M&A under Biden. (The
agencies took lead roles in blocking the Jet Blue-Spirit merger and Lockhee=
d Martin=E2=80=99s purchase of Aerojet Rocketdyne.) If Kennedy can position=
 himself as a champion of America=E2=80=99s health=E2=80=94still a big if=
=E2=80=94his new perch could allow him greater leverage to intercede in a m=
ultitude of ways.<br/><br/>Finally, HHS is a cornerstone in federal scienti=
fic research, a foundation often utilized by journalists covering major ins=
titutions and corporate behaviors. If Kennedy were to tamper with this rese=
arch=E2=80=94by curtailing it, limiting access, or skewing it to fit partic=
ular ideological leanings=E2=80=94it could erode the factual bedrock upon w=
hich journalists build their stories. That, in turn, could expose media ent=
ities to libel risks, as the defensibility of their coverage could be compr=
omised.<br/></td>
         </tr>

         <tr class=3D"replica" name=3D"s17">
          <td style=3D"padding-bottom: 36px; color: #000000; font-size: 16p=
x; line-height: 30px; margin-top: 0; margin-bottom: 0;">Of course, no one k=
nows what Trump 2.0 will bring. Some fear we=E2=80=99re headed into a new, =
autocratic era, but I think we=E2=80=99re bound for something less dystopia=
n but potentially more fraught: an atmosphere in which media entities retai=
n independence and perhaps even flourish financially, and yet proceed cauti=
ously while prioritizing self-preservation, becoming less daring and effect=
ive in the process. There=E2=80=99s some basis for this outcome in the medi=
a landscape of the U.K., a region of fascinating contradictions. <br/><br/>=
Britain is renowned for =E2=80=9Clibel tourism,=E2=80=9D thanks to defamati=
on laws heavily skewed in favor of the plaintiff. Journalists often find th=
emselves in the precarious position of having to substantiate truth in cour=
t, as opposed to their subjects having to demonstrate falsity in the U.S. M=
oreover, the U.K. pioneered the use of
super-injunctions, a legal maneuver that sometimes prevents the press from =
even detailing allegations against powerful figures. In other words, it=E2=
=80=99s a dangerous country to publish anything sensitive that might tarnis=
h someone=E2=80=99s reputation.<br/><br/>Ironically, alongside this restric=
tive legal framework thrives a vibrant tabloid culture. British publication=
s famously pursue celebrities and sensationalize gossip, prioritizing scand=
al over substance and inflaming class and racial distinctions, while relyin=
g on the relatively modest damages awarded in successful libel suits in the=
 U.K., and potential landmines lurking in discovery. This discourages all b=
ut the most well-funded targets. Despite the tabloids=E2=80=99 commercial s=
uccess, this is not the kind of journalism that cements a reputation as a f=
ormidable fourth estate.<br/><br/>Maybe it=E2=80=99s a window into Trump 2.=
0: fewer investigative outlets to hold power to account, but more <i>entert=
ainment</i>. You might even call it
Murdochian. <br/></td>
